### Good Scientific Practice 
Module 1
to the interdisciplinary online learning programme "Good Academic Practice during Doctoral Studies"!  
   
In this learning programme, we will explain what you should consider in order to be successful with your doctoral studies.   

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080031726046214171/pages/P1/image.bee7623d596d492aa630b81f500021ce.png?1762186768279)

Four modules are available to you after the chapter on getting started:

- **Background**  
Here you will learn about what characterizes good academic practice and what kinds of misconduct there are.   

- **Orientation Phase**  
This chapter explains how you can successfully start your doctoral studies and which legal aspects  
are relevant to you.  

- **Working Phase**  
You will receive important information regarding how to organize your literature and research, how to correctly cite text and images and how to save your results.

**Completion Phase**  
The last chapter describes at what point you are considered to be an author of a publication and how the publication process works.  
  
This first module "**Getting Started**" 
- offers you an overview of the current learning programme
- helps you to understand the navigation and the learning objective
For completing the following modules, you will need approximately 15 minutes per module.Navigate 
open page directly light blue unopened , white iopen current page dark blue. or use navigation arrows
Additional page via link 
Academic Misconduct

Recommendation of the 185th plenum of the German Rectors' Conference from 6 July 1998  
(direct translation from German)

"Academic misconduct is said to occur when, in a scientifically important context, intentional or grossly negligent false information is given, the intellectual property of others is infringed upon or their research work is otherwise affected. The circumstances of the individual case play a decisive role.

  
In particular, the following can be considered examples of possible serious misconduct:  
  
a. false information [...]  
b. infringing on intellectual property [...]  
c. claiming the (co-)authorship of another without his or her consent [...]  
d. sabotaging research work (including damaging, destroying or manipulating experimental set-ups, devices, documents, hardware, software, chemicals or other things that another needs to carry out an experiment) [...]  
e. disposing of primary data [..], insofar as this violates legal provisions or discipline-related accepted principles of academic work."

C

Circulation Period

The time during which your doctoral work is available for the internal university community to evaluate after the submission of your dissertation is known as the circulation period.

  
"The right to inspection" (university-wide doctoral regulations § 9(5)) is specified in the faculty-specific doctoral regulations.

Cumulative Dissertation

Dissertations can consist of multiple previously published manuscripts as well as manuscripts that are soon to be in print. This type of publication is known as the cumulative or publication-based dissertation. Here it is important to clearly indicate the individual contribution of the doctoral candidate, for example, by providing information regarding the individual work contributed to each publication.

  
Previously published manuscripts are to be cited within the cumulative dissertation. The reverse is also true: unpublished manuscripts from the cumulative dissertation that are published at a later time must include a citation that refers to the dissertation.

D

Disputation

The disputation is the oral final examination of doctoral studies. The doctoral candidates present their doctoral projects and defend them in front of a expert audience. The strengths and also the weaknesses of the doctoral project are worked out and discussed.

  
See also "Rigorosum".

Doctoral Committee

according to § 2 of the GU university-wide doctoral regulations

  
Details about doctoral committees are specified in the doctoral regulations of the faculties. The faculties are responsible for the realization and implementation of the doctoral studies, in particular, for

  
a) accepting the doctoral student  
b) initiating the examination process  
c) appointing the referees  
d) forming the examination committee

  
Getting Started

LE 1 - 1
    
 - 2
    
 - 3
    
 - 4
    
 - 5
    

## M1

### On the
  
The doctoral committee is composed of at least

  
a) three professors  
b) one research associate  
c) one student (at least in undergraduate, master's or doctoral studies)

  
The professors must make up the majority. Several faculties can together form a joint doctoral committee.

Doctoral Relationship

The doctoral relationship formally begins with the acceptance as a doctoral student in the faculty. From this point in time, the faculty-specific doctoral regulations apply.

However, during the admission procedure, there are already certain criteria that must be met, for example, providing proof of your completed university studies and specifying your supervisor.

E

Employment Relationship

The employment relationship is the contractually stipulated legal relationship between an employee and his or her employer.  
  
Doctoral candidates should clearly distinguish between the employment relationship and the research and supervisory relationship.

  
Among other things, the work contract regulates

  
● the job requirements (for example, the extent of the teaching obligations)  
● the length of employment  
● the number of working days, holidays and paid leave  
● the salary

Examination Committee

according to § 11 of the GU university-wide doctoral regulations

  
The examination committee is appointed by the doctoral committee and determines the candidate's performance during doctoral studies in a closed session.

  
The committee generally consists of the referees as well as an additional professor from the faculty of the doctoral candidate. The dissertation and the evaluations (and, if applicable, other documents) shall be made available to them.

  
In the case of cotutelle doctorates – binationally supervised doctoral studies at at least two universities – there are additional particularities. Further details can be found in the respective doctoral regulations for the faculty.

H

Hypothesis

A hypothesis is a scientific supposition. One can derive it inductively from acquiring knowledge through reading or observations or deduce it from laws or theories. Hypotheses are systematically and intersubjectively checked in a way that is comprehensible to others.

  
Depending on the particular scientific theoretical approach, at the end of the process, the hypothesis is rejected (falsifiability), verified (verifiability) or modified.

I

Inductive Approach

Academic knowledge is not only gained from checking expectations and hypotheses (deduction), but also from general conclusions drawn from observed phenomena and working through literature (induction). Some examples for inductively gained knowledge are the discovery of natural laws and the development of superordinate terms and theories.

P

Primary Literature

from Yale University Library Subject Guide ["Comparative Literature"](http://guides.library.yale.edu/content.php?pid=128822&sid=1187254) [accessed 10/12/2014]

"Primary sources are original materials on which other research is based. They are from the time period involved and have not been filtered through interpretation or evaluation. They are usually the first formal appearance of results in physical, print or electronic format. They present original thinking, report a discovery, or share new information."

"Secondary sources are less easily defined than primary sources. Generally, they are accounts written after the fact with the benefit of hindsight. They are interpretations and evaluations of primary sources. Secondary sources are not evidence, but rather commentary on and discussion of evidence. However, what some define as a secondary source, others define as a tertiary source. Context is everything."

R

Rigorosum

A rigorosum replaces the disputation as an oral final examination at some universities. Different than in a disputation, in a rigorosum, a critical stance not only adopted towards the dissertation topic, but other subjects are tested as well.

S

Search Alerts

Many online databases and search engines offer the possibility of receiving regular news about keywords (or a combination of keywords) by e-mail. These search alerts could be used, for example, to stay informed about the latest relevant research results. Some examples of this are PubMed Central and Google Alerts.

Supervisory Relationship

The supervisory relationship regulates the formal relationship between the doctoral candidates and their doctoral study supervisors. In general, both sides sign a joint supervisory agreement.

  
Among other things, the agreement covers

  
● times for regular meetings  
● length of doctoral studies  
● language of the dissertation  
● financial and professional support  
● latitude for qualification outside the subject area  
● talks about career perspectives

T

Touch-up Tools

Touch-up tools is the term used for the settings in graphics programmes that, as opposed to processing the overall image (for example, contrast or brightness), only make specific changes. In image editing within the framework of academic publications, such changes should be avoided or clearly specified.

  
Touch-up tools include, among others, cloning or healing tools. These programmes make it possible to replace one part of the image with another.

Certificate: GRADE – Goethe Graduate Academy
You have decided to complete doctoral studies, probably your first independent research – and you want to get started right away.

Before doing so, it is important to pause for a moment.

**Please keep in mind that there are rules that you absolutely must follow to be successful.**

This chapter informs you about the guidelines of the academic community; the ethical and technical guidelines for academic work as well as the consequences in the case of misconduct.
### Science is Comprehensible to Others

Research aims at gaining objective or intersubjective insights.

Researchers test hypotheses in a systematic and revisable way in order to gain new insights. Or conclusions are drawn from observations (inductive approach).

In publications, they document and publish the research results so that other researchers can comprehend their work and express their views regarding it.
### Research Findings Build up on Each Other

Research begins with an innovative question. Based on the current state of knowledge, expectations are formulated and tested. The research methods depend on the type of question and the discipline.

All research is integrated within an existing academic context and is part of a continuing process. In the course of this, every discovery is tied to the knowledge gained from previous work. This was already pointed out in the year 1120 by Bernard of Chartres in a parable about giants and dwarves (quote ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P4/Link_intern.png)):        

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P4/image.3999451989554f5caddfc3383d7cb0af.png?1762188814587)

Dwarves can see further, because they are standing on the shoulders of giants.
### Good Academic Practice

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P6/image.e767d130a78d4153b0f20933118bbd03.png?1762188814589)

Academic research is only successful when society trusts those doing the research. This in turn requires good academic practice.  
   
During your doctoral studies, you must therefore

  

- honestly describe where and how you refer to other works
- document your results
- save your results and store them  

You should reflect on 

  

- how research results are collected and how theses are formulated and discussed in a way that others can understand 
- how to save and publish results
- when an authorship is justified
- how to cite correctly
- what is protected by copyright
- ### System of Self-monitoring

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/image.19db40bbab194ada8601924af9d15428.png?1762188814589)

Through individual misconduct, you not only damage your own reputation but also the reputation of your supervisors, the institute, the university, the discipline and the entire field of academic research. An example of this is the plagiarism case of Karl-Theodor zu Guttenberg ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/Link_intern.png).        

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/image.beedeacc1b0c421b885f2e6b7ea55129.png?1762188814590)

The fraud case of Herrmann/Brach ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/Link_extern.png)prompted the German Research Foundation (the DFG) in 1998 to formulate recommendations for "Saveguarding Good Scientific Practice" ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/Link_intern.png).

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P7/image.4dadde13d5d84f41a3afd362da60fcda.png?1762188814591)

Under these recommendations, institutions as well as academic researchers commit to selfmonitoring.  
   
In reaction to this fraud case, the German Rectors' Conference (HRK) also dealt with

academic misconduct in 1998 and defined it.
### Saveguarding Good Scientific Practice

![](data:image/png;base64,) Good Scientific Practice – Report from the DFG (accessed 29 June 2020)

"The aim of the Code is to enable the intended audience – researchers and heads of universities and non-university research institutions – to align their internal structures, processes and actions with the guidelines for good research practice. This is intended to create a deeply rooted culture of research integrity at higher education institutions, focusing less on breaches of good scientific practice and more on the professional ethics of researchers.

As of 1 August 2019, all universities and non-university research institutions will be required to implement the 19 guidelines in a legally binding manner to be eligible to receive DFG funding. For those universities and non-university research institutions that have already implemented the relevant requirements in the DFG white paper “Safeguarding Good Scientific Practice” in a binding manner, there is a two-year transition period for implementing the guidelines in the Code.”

Guidelines for Safeguarding Good Research Practice - Code of Conduct ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P8/Link_intern.png) (PDF)  
       

DFG: ![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P8/Link_extern.png) Research Ombudsman

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P8/Link_extern.png) Details regarding the procedures of the DFG        

![](data:image/png;base64,) Symposium of the Alliance of German Science Organisations on "Good Scientific Practice" (in German)
### In the Event of Suspected Plagiarism

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P17/image.dba7b3056f5e4bc59cf5aaadb67f2a06.png?1762188814594)

If colleagues do not follow the rules for good academic practice or if you suspect plagiarism, the DFG recommends a two-step approach to clarify the situation:  

**Step 1: The Informal Preliminary Investigation**  
Document the incident and inform the ombudspersons of the institution concerned or the project managers.  
It is important that the charge be treated confidentially and that none of the parties are made public – neither the whistleblower nor the accused person.  
   
**Step 2: The Formal Process**  
The ombudspersons arrange hearings of evidence and listen to the testimony of the involved people. Finally, measures are formulated.    

If you should have a strong suspicion, please contact the appropriate contact persons.   

  

- Supervisors
- The responsible ombudspersons  ![](data:image/png;base64,) at your institution 

In their policy regarding good scientific practice  ![](data:image/png;base64,)  [grade:text2_11_1] recommendations of the HRK (1998)  ![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Consequences of Misconduct

Academic misconduct can be sanctioned in varied ways:

  

  

- **Academic**  
    

  

Withdrawal of an academic title or the authorisation to teach, ban on applications for funding, retraction of publications  

- **Labour law**  
    

  

Disciplinary measures, warning, termination of employment, cancellation of a contract  

- **Criminal law**  
    

  

For example, in the cases of  
document forgery and fraud  

- **Civil law**  
    

  

Exclusion order, repayment claims for third-party funding and stipends, compensation for damages   

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P12/image.2a5dad43cfa747669df494b4a67d57a1.png?1762188814594)

One example of serious consequences is a case from the Netherlands that became known in 2011:

The psychologist Diederik Stapel ![](data:image/png;base64,) admitted to fabricating and manipulating research data on a massive scale.        

After his offences became known, the University of Tilburg suspended him from his position as a professor. Afterwards, Stapel voluntarily gave his doctor degree back to the University of Amsterdam.

So far 50 of his publications have been retracted.
### Teaching Responsibly

In the course of your doctoral studies, you will sometimes find yourself in an academic double role: On the one hand, you will work independently on an original research project. On the other hand, you will supervise students and introduce them to the academic community.

It is therefore important that you know the guidelines for good academic practice, apply them reliably and impart them to the students.        

![](https://bildungsportal.sachsen.de/scormruntime/course-packages/HOT_OPAL-461742080041726046279124/pages/P13/image.65678c34a2834b67a679417e28609268.jpg?1762188814595)

  

For your preparation, you should use the Curriculum for Courses in "Good Scientific Practice" ![](data:image/png;base64,) , which the DFG commission "**Ombudsman für die Wissenschaft**" designed.

The universities, the individual supervisors, but also you yourself are responsible for incorporating academic ethical standards in day-to-day research and setting an example by following them.
### Supervision Agreement

Your doctoral studies comprise an independent research project. Yet you do not work alone: you are supported by supervisors. Speak with your supervisor about central topics of good academic practice that are relevant to your doctoral studies.  
   
A good supervisory relationship supports not only your research work, but also prevents academic misconduct.
### Summary

In this chapter, you have learned

- the characteristics of good academic practice
- where you can find the relevant guidelines
- the types of academic misconduct
- the possible consequences of academic misconduct
- why supervision agreements make sense

**Additional Literature and Links**

The Plagiarism Advice Web Page ![](data:image/png;base64,) provides helpful background information and educational materials on the topic of "Avoiding Plagiarism".

The Singapore Statement on Research Integrity ![](data:image/png;base64,) describes the first efforts to establish uniform international guidelines on academic ethics.

The European Charta for Researchers ![](data:image/png;base64,) is a code of conduct for researchers and their employers or research institutions in Europe.

Inform yourself about the guidelines of the largest German funding providers, for example, the German Research Foundation (DFG ![](data:image/png;base64,)), the German Federal Ministry for Education and Research (BMBF ![](data:image/png;base64,)) and the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD ![](data:image/png;base64,)).

The commission „Ombudsman für die Wissenschaft“ ![](data:image/png;base64,) which was introduced by a DFG-appointed commission, handles cases of academic misconduct throughout Germany and reports about them.
